claim: For all integers n>=1, (1+2+...+n)^2 equals 1^3+2^3+...+n^3.
predicate: (n<1) or (sum(range(1,n+1))**2 == sum(i**3 for i in range(1,n+1)))

claim: For all n>=1, 1+2+...+n equals n(n+1)/2.
For all n>=1, n(n+1) is always even.
For all n>=1, the triangular number n(n+1)/2 is divisible by n if n+1 is even, i.e. if n is odd.
For all n>=1, the sum of cubes 1^3+...+n^3 is a perfect square.
predicate: (n<1) or (int(math.isqrt(sum(i**3 for i in range(1,n+1))))**2 == sum(i**3 for i in range(1,n+1)))

claim: For all n>=1, the triangular number T(n)=n(n+1)/2 is an integer (equivalently n(n+1) is even, always true).
For all n>=1, T(n) mod 3 cycles with period 3: T(n)%3 in {0,1,0} depending on n%3 pattern, specifically T(n)%3==0 iff n%3 in {0,2}.
For all n>=1, sum of cubes 1^3+...+n^3 is divisible by 4 whenever n is even.
predicate: (n<1) or (n%2!=0) or (sum(i**3 for i in range(1,n+1))%4==0)

claim: For all n>=1, sum of cubes 1^3+...+n^3 is odd iff both n and n+1 halved appropriately make T(n) odd, i.e. sum of cubes is odd iff n%4 in {1,2}.
For all n>=1, if n is odd then T(n)/n = (n+1)/2 is an integer and equals the average of 1 and n+1.
For all n>=1, T(n) - T(n-1) equals n.
For all n>=1, the difference of consecutive cube-sum-squares (T(n)^2 - T(n-1)^2) equals n^3.
For all n>=1, T(n) is divisible by n(n+1)/gcd(2,n(n+1)) trivially, more specifically gcd(T(n), n) equals n if n is odd and n/2 if n is even.
For all n>=1, T(n) is a multiple of (n+1) exactly when n is even.
For all n>=1, gcd(T(n), n+1) equals (n+1) if n even, and (n+1)/2 if n odd.
For all n>=1, 8*T(n)+1 is a perfect square.
For all n>=1, 8*T(n)+1 equals (2n+1)^2.
For all n>=1, T(n) is divisible by n+1 or by n, but not necessarily both, except when n%3==0 additional divisibility by 3 also holds via T(n)%3==0.
For all n>=1, T(2n) equals n(2n+1).
For all n>=1, T(2n-1) equals n^2 + n(n-1) i.e. simplifies to n(2n-1).
For all n>=1, T(2n) - T(2n-1) equals 2n.
For all n>=1, sum of cubes 1^3+...+(2n)^3 equals n^2(2n+1)^2.
For all n>=1, sum of cubes 1^3+...+(2n)^3 is divisible by 4.
For all n>=1, sum of cubes 1^3+...+(2n-1)^3 equals n^2(2n-1)^2.
For all n>=1, the sum of cubes from (2n-1)^3 to (2n)^3 (i.e. two consecutive terms) equals n^2(2n+1)^2 - n^2(2n-1)^2.
For all n>=1, (2n-1)^3+(2n)^3 simplifies to n^2*(8n) i.e. equals 8n^3.
For all n>=1, gcd(T(n), 2n+1) divides 1, i.e. T(n) and 2n+1 are always coprime.
For all n>=1, phi(T(n)) is even whenever T(n) > 2.
For all n>=1, tau(T(n)) is odd iff T(n) is a perfect square.
For all n>=1, T(n) is a perfect square only for n in a sparse set (e.g. n=1,8,49,...), so for most n tau(T(n)) is even.

For all n>=1, sigma(n) + sigma(n+1) is not generally equal to sigma(T(n)), i.e. sigma is not additive across the triangular number construction (this is expected to fail, testing structural non-multiplicativity) — restated positively: sigma(T(n)) is at least sigma(n)+sigma(n+1)-1.
For all n>=1, exactly one of n, n+1 is even, and T(n) = (even one)/2 * (odd one).
For all n>=1 with n odd, T(n) equals n times (n+1)/2, and gcd(n,(n+1)/2) equals 1 or a small common factor only when n shares factor with n+1)/2 which cannot happen since n and n+1 are coprime.
For all n>=1 with n even, T(n) equals (n/2) times (n+1), and gcd(n/2,n+1) equals 1.
For all n>=1, T(n) is squarefree-factor-compatible in that its two coprime factors (as in nodes 33/34) each individually determine tau(T(n)) as a product tau(factor1)*tau(factor2).
For all n>=1, since T(n) factors into two coprime parts, phi(T(n)) equals phi(of the two coprime factors) multiplied together.
For all n>=1, since T(n) factors into two coprime parts, sigma(T(n)) equals sigma of the two coprime factors multiplied together.
For all n>=1, T(n) mod n equals 0 if n odd and n/2 if n even.
For all n>=1, T(n) is never divisible by n when n is even (n>=2).
For all n>=1, T(n) is divisible by 3 iff n%3 is 0 or 2.
For all n>=1, T(n) mod 9 has period 9 in n (i.e. T(n+9) ≡ T(n) mod 9).
For all n>=1, T(n) mod 5 has period 5.
For all n>=1, T(n) is divisible by 15 iff (n%3 in {0,2}) and (n%5 in {0,4}).
For all n>=1, tau(sum of cubes 1^3+...+n^3) is always odd, since the sum of cubes is a perfect square.

For all n>=1, phi(sum of cubes) equals phi(T(n))^2 / T(n) times T(n), simplification: phi(T(n)^2) equals T(n)*phi(T(n)) when T(n)'s prime factors are same for T(n) and T(n)^2 (always true), i.e. phi(T(n)^2)=T(n)*phi(T(n)).

For all n>=1, sigma(T(n)^2) is odd iff T(n)^2 is a perfect square times an odd condition — specifically sigma(T(n)^2) is always odd since T(n)^2 is a perfect square and sigma of a perfect square with all even exponents... actually sigma(m) is odd iff m is a square or twice a square; T(n)^2 is always a square so sigma(T(n)^2) is odd.
For all n>=1, gcd(2n+1, T(n)) being 1 (node 27) combined with 8T(n)+1=(2n+1)^2 (node 17) implies 2n+1 is coprime to T(n) but shares total square relationship, i.e. gcd((2n+1)^2, T(n)) also equals 1.
For all n>=1, T(n) is odd iff n%4 is 1 or 2.
For all n>=1, the sum of cubes 1^3+...+n^3 is odd exactly when T(n) is odd (since it's T(n)^2, parity of T(n)^2 matches parity of T(n)).
For all n>=1, the two parity characterizations of the sum of cubes (via n%4 in {1,2} from node 9, and via T(n) parity from node 49) are consistent with each other.
